point_001: The inference batch size used in experiments is never specified, and it is unclear how performance varies with different batch sizes, which is a critical parameter since predictions for a datapoint depend on other samples in the same batch.
point_002: The threat model is unrealistic: experiments appear to assume entire test batches consist of the same type of adversarial attack, whereas real-world scenarios involve mixed batches containing both clean and adversarially perturbed samples, or multiple different attack types simultaneously.
point_003: No comparison is provided between the robustness of lightweight/parameter-efficient tuning methods (e.g., prefix-tuning, adapters) and full fine-tuning, making it unclear whether lightweight methods are inherently more vulnerable to adversarial attacks.
point_004: The paper lacks comparison with full fine-tuning baselines (with and without defenses), making it impossible to contextualize how competitive the proposed method's robustness numbers actually are against established defense approaches.
point_005: Section 5 (qualitative attention analysis) is over-represented in the main body given that it relies on attention weights, which are known to be unreliable as explanatory tools (attention is not necessarily faithful to model decisions), and this limitation is not adequately acknowledged.
point_006: Section 6 (optimal control / theoretical interpretation) is poorly positioned at the end of the paper after the experiments, making it disconnected from the rest of the narrative; it should either be moved before the experiments or relegated to the appendix.
point_007: Several mathematical notations are undefined or insufficiently explained, for example the variable X_C appears in Eq. 4 without prior definition, and Eq. 8 (the core optimization objective) lacks sufficient accompanying explanation.
point_008: The attention visualization figures (e.g., Figure 4) show tokens attending to themselves, which is unexpected for a standard language model; the figure captions also fail to explain what rows and columns represent, hindering interpretability.
point_009: Dataset statistics such as number of classes, random-baseline accuracy, and majority-class accuracy are not reported alongside results, making it difficult to interpret scores (e.g., a score matching random-chance accuracy after defense is not meaningful improvement).
point_010: The paper does not evaluate the method on text generation tasks, where adversarial attacks can cause qualitatively more harmful outputs than mere misclassification, limiting the demonstrated scope and impact of the approach.
point_011: Related parameter-efficient transfer learning methods such as adapters, P-tuning, and standard prompt tuning are not discussed, leaving the related work section incomplete.
point_012: The writing quality needs improvement throughout the paper, including grammatical errors and awkward phrasing (e.g., 'remaining the pretrained models unmodified'), and the bibliography contains formatting inconsistencies such as incorrect capitalization of model names and conference titles.
